paragraphs.text: So for weakly binding alkali ions EDTA still chelates measurably; for harder and transition-metal ions, the catalog shows extensive additional systems, including **Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺, Cd²⁺, Cu²⁺, Ni²⁺, Zn²⁺, Co²⁺, Hg²⁺, Fe³⁺, Al³⁺, lanthanides, Th⁴⁺, VO₂⁺, and UO₂²⁺**.

for harder and transition-metal ions, the catalog shows extensive additional systems
**Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺, Cd²⁺, Cu²⁺, Ni²⁺, Zn²⁺, Co²⁺, Hg²⁺, Fe³⁺, Al³⁺, lanthanides, Th⁴⁺, VO₂⁺, and UO₂²⁺**

paragraphs.text:
| Qualitative conclusion | Evidence from SRD-46 |
|---|---|
| Broad-spectrum chelator | 75 metal partners |
| Forms many defined species | 20 metal/ligand systems, 66 species in catalog |
| pH-sensitive but strong when deprotonated | Full protonation ladder through H4L/HL/L |
| Especially useful from neutral to basic pH | Higher deprotonation above pKa 6.13 and 9.52 frees donor sites |

claims.text: | Forms many defined species | 20 metal/ligand systems, 66 species in catalog |
| pH-sensitive but strong when deprotonated | Full protonation ladder through H4L/HL/L |
| Especially useful from neutral to basic pH | Higher deprotonation above pKa 6.13 and 9.52 frees donor sites |

paragraphs.text: **Bottom line:** EDTA is an **excellent, broadly useful chelator**, especially at **neutral to basic pH**, where more of the ligand is deprotonated and available to bind metals strongly. In acidic solution it is still a chelator, but its effectiveness drops because protonation competes with metal binding.

claims.text: EDTA is an **excellent, broadly useful chelator**,
where more of the ligand is deprotonated
In acidic solution it is still a chelator,
because protonation competes with metal binding.
